analogy: Think of "Rulers and Buildings" through the chapter's own opening images: 49 TRIBES, NOMADS AND SETTLED COMMUNITIES You saw in Chapters 2, 3 and 4 how kingdoms rose and fell. Even as this was happening, new arts, and villages. Over the centuries important political, social and economic developments had taken place. But social change was not... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Rulers and Buildings is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that 49 TRIBES, NOMADS AND SETTLED COMMUNITIES You saw in Chapters 2, 3 and 4 how kingdoms rose and fell.

The text explains that even as this was happening, new arts, and villages.

The text explains that over the centuries important political, social and economic developments had taken place.

The text explains that but social change was not the same everywhere, because different kinds of societies evolved differently.

The text explains that it is important to understand how and why this happened.

The text explains that in large parts of the subcontinent, society was already divided according to the rules of .

The text explains that these rules, as prescribed by the Brahmanas, were accepted by the rulers of large kingdoms.

The text explains that the difference between the high and low, and between the rich and poor, increased.

The text explains that under the Delhi Sultans and the Mughals, this hierarchy between social classes grew further.

The text explains that beyond Big Cities: Tribal Societies There were, however, other kinds of societies as well.

The text explains that many societies in the subcontinent did not follow the social rules and rituals prescribed by the Brahmanas.

The text explains that nor were they divided into numerous unequal classes.

The text explains that 50 Members of each tribe were united by kinship bonds.

The text explains that many tribes obtained their livelihood from agriculture.

The text explains that most often they combined these activities to make full use of the natural resources of the area in which they lived.

The text explains that some tribes were nomadic and moved from one place to another.

The text explains that but direct Mughal control over the region could not last long.

The text explains that 60 (d) The Bhils lived in the north-eastern part of the subcontinent.

The text explains that what kinds of exchanges took place between nomadic pastoralists and settled agriculturists?

The text explains that how was the administration of the Ahom state organised?

The text explains that how did tribal societies change after being organised into a state?

The text explains that in what ways was the history of the Gonds different from that of the Ahoms?

The text explains that plot the location of the tribes mentioned in this chapter on a map.

The text explains that for any two, discuss whether their mode of livelihood was suited to the geography and the environment of the area where they lived.

The text explains that find out about present-day government policies towards tribal populations and organise a discussion about these.

The text explains that find out more about present-day nomadic pastoral groups in the subcontinent.

The text explains that which are the areas frequented by these groups?

Another source point is that even as this was happening, new arts, and villages.

prelims_traps: Do not treat 49 TRIBES, NOMADS AND and SETTLED COMMUNITIES as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Rulers and Buildings; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Rulers and Buildings through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 49 TRIBES, NOMADS AND and SETTLED COMMUNITIES to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
